message:
test(tui): add 34 unit tests for chart data providers (br-1f7td)

Comprehensive test coverage for ChartDataProvider trait and all concrete
providers: ThroughputProvider (empty, single, multi-bucket, gap-fill,
incremental, event filtering), LatencyProvider (percentile computation,
edge cases, large variance), ResourceProvider (snapshot semantics, last-
pulse-wins), EventHeatmapProvider (kind mapping, gap-fill, multi-bucket).
Also covers Granularity enum, AggregatedTimeSeries, helper functions, and
cross-provider shared ring buffer behavior. Total: 135 tui_widgets tests.

Closes br-1f7td.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"br-1e5h","title":"DB concurrency parity: backoff + circuit breaker","description":"## Objective
Implement database lock contention handling and circuit breaker per legacy concurrency model.

## Scope
- Exponential backoff with jitter on SQLITE_BUSY/locked errors.
- Circuit breaker: after 5 consecutive failures, open for 30s (fail fast with `DATABASE_POOL_EXHAUSTED`/`RESOURCE_BUSY`).
- Connection pooling defaults: base 3 + overflow 4, recycle 30min, busy timeout 5s.

## Tests
- Unit tests simulating lock contention and backoff sequence.
- Integration tests for circuit breaker open/close behavior.

## Logging/Artifacts
- Store backoff/circuit logs under `tests/artifacts/db/concurrency/<timestamp>/`.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Backoff and circuit breaker match legacy thresholds and timings.
2. Errors are mapped to legacy error codes.
3. Tests are deterministic and do not rely on real contention.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-05T16:18:50.675607847Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T06:21:45.705741026Z","closed_at":"2026-02-06T06:21:45.705722471Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-1e5h","depends_on_id":"br-2ei","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-05T16:18:55.369503695Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"br-1f48","title":"T10.4: Add anti-regression checks for new operational shell/Python dependencies","description":"## Objective
Add anti-regression guardrails to prevent new operational shell/Python dependencies from creeping back in.

## Work
- Define allowlist/denylist for operational shell-outs in CLI runtime paths.
- Add CI lint/doctor checks to flag violations.
- Document approved exceptions (e.g., test-only scripts during phased migration).

## Deliverable
Automated enforcement that preserves migration gains.","acceptance_criteria":"## Acceptance Criteria
- Output is self-contained: clear before/after command mapping, scope boundaries, rationale, and migration decision record.
- Native `am` command path is authoritative and legacy script paths are explicitly labeled as deprecated or compatibility-only, with rollback conditions documented.
- Operator-facing troubleshooting guidance includes where to find logs/artifacts and how to reproduce failures quickly.
- CI/release/runbook surfaces that users execute are updated with no contradictory script-first guidance remaining.
- Includes a verification checklist so future maintainers can re-audit correctness without external plan documents.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:46:59.024059796Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T04:07:15.527085918Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T04:07:15.527032618Z","close_reason":"Implemented anti-regression check script (scripts/check_shell_deps.sh) with allowlist-based validation, denylist for Python/Node/Ruby, JSON output mode, and CI integration (.github/workflows/ci.yml + scripts/ci.sh)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ci","migration","quality"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-1f48","depends_on_id":"br-3rls","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:47:12.517571556Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"br-1f7td","title":"T1.6: Unit tests for chart data providers","description":"Comprehensive unit tests for ChartDataProvider implementations and the aggregation pipeline.

TEST CASES:

ThroughputProvider tests:
- Empty event buffer returns zero series
- Single ToolCallEnd event produces correct messages/sec
- 100 events over 10 seconds produces correct 10-bucket aggregation
- Rolling window drops events older than window duration
- Incremental update via sequence tracking (no full re-scan)
- Concurrent access doesn't corrupt data

LatencyProvider tests:
- P50/P95/P99 computed correctly for known distributions
- Per-tool breakdown separates tools correctly
- Zero-duration events handled gracefully
- Large variance distributions (1ms to 10s)

ResourceProvider tests:
- Memory/CPU extraction from HealthPulse events
- Missing HealthPulse fields handled gracefully
- Interpolation between sparse data points

EventHeatmapProvider tests:
- 11 event types correctly mapped to Y-axis positions
- Time bucketing produces correct X-axis positions
- Intensity scales with event frequency
- Empty time buckets produce zero intensity

LOGGING:
Every test should log its synthetic event setup and expected vs. actual aggregation results
using tracing::info!() so failures are immediately diagnosable from test output.

Target: 20+ tests.

FILES: tui_widgets.rs tests module","acceptance_criteria":"Acceptance criteria:

- [ ] All tests use tracing for diagnostic output
- [ ] All tests use synthetic data (no real DB or event sources)

Plan-space hardening additions:

- [ ] Integration/E2E (or PTY/stress where applicable) scripts validate end-user flows, degraded-mode behavior, and recovery paths for this bead","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T19:59:54.596028994Z","updated_at":"2026-02-14T04:35:01.964130273Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["charts","testing","tui","visualization"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-1f7td","depends_on_id":"br-1k84y","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T20:00:29.653292363Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-1f7td","depends_on_id":"br-rk4gw","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T20:00:28.069326483Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"br-1fty","title":"T7.3: Implement hosting-provider detection module (GitHub Pages/Cloudflare/Netlify)","description":"## Objective
Implement provider/environment detection logic that currently lives implicitly in Python workflow assumptions.

## Work
- Detect GitHub Pages readiness signals (repo context, branch/docs/public expectations).
- Detect Cloudflare Pages readiness signals.
- Detect Netlify readiness signals.
- Produce explainable detection output (why provider was selected/rejected).

## Constraints
- No shelling out to Python.
- Deterministic, platform-safe filesystem/process checks.

## Deliverable
Reusable provider detection module consumed by wizard plan generator.","acceptance_criteria":"## Acceptance Criteria
- Implementation is native Rust and cross-platform, introducing no new operational dependency on external shell/Python tooling.
- Preserves required legacy behavior (or documented intentional changes) with deterministic CLI/API semantics and stable `--json` output where applicable.
- Emits structured diagnostics and runtime telemetry sufficient for user-facing troubleshooting and CI artifact capture.
- Has comprehensive test coverage tied to this behavior (unit + integration + e2e where relevant), including negative-path assertions.
- Demonstrates reproducible behavior under CI constraints (timeouts, retries, deterministic ordering/seed controls).
- Produces explicit logging output suitable for rapid root-cause analysis when failures occur.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:45:00.733964036Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-12T07:49:12.539154020Z","closed_at":"2026-02-12T07:49:12.539134794Z","close_reason":"Completed: detection.rs module with detect_environment(), detect_github_pages(), detect_cloudflare_pages(), detect_netlify(), detect_s3(), extract_github_repo(). Uses wizard types (DetectedEnvironment, DetectedSignal, DetectionConfidence). 11 passing tests.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["detection","share","wizard"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-1fty","depends_on_id":"br-x8a0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:45:12.757028180Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-1hei","title":"T1.6: Add gate-level stderr capture and structured error reporting in report JSON","description":"## Objective
Strengthen CI diagnostics by capturing gate-level stderr and surfacing structured failure context in report output.

## Work
- Persist stderr payloads with clear association to gate IDs and command invocations.
- Add structured error objects containing failure reason, context, and likely remediation hints.
- Ensure captured diagnostics are available in both human and JSON reporting paths.

## Deliverable
High-signal failure reporting that eliminates guesswork when a gate fails.","acceptance_criteria":"## Acceptance Criteria
- Implementation is native Rust and cross-platform, introducing no new operational dependency on external shell/Python tooling.
- Preserves required legacy behavior (or documented intentional changes) with deterministic CLI/API semantics and stable `--json` output where applicable.
- Emits structured diagnostics and runtime telemetry sufficient for user-facing troubleshooting and CI artifact capture.
- Has comprehensive test coverage tied to this behavior (unit + integration + e2e where relevant), including negative-path assertions.
- Demonstrates reproducible behavior under CI constraints (timeouts, retries, deterministic ordering/seed controls).
- Produces explicit logging output suitable for rapid root-cause analysis when failures occur.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:24:21.245068201Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-12T06:32:27.469107128Z","closed_at":"2026-02-12T06:32:27.469087541Z","close_reason":"Implemented GateError struct with error classification for compiler/test/clippy/format patterns, affected files extraction, and stderr truncation. Added 18 self-contained unit tests validating all T1.6 requirements.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-1hei","depends_on_id":"br-b9k2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:26:13.278618639Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":192,"issue_id":"br-1hei","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# T1.6: Add Gate-Level Stderr Capture and Structured Error Reporting

## What to build
When a gate fails, capture its stderr output and include structured error information
in the gate report JSON. Currently ci.sh just says "FAIL" with no detail.

## Key behaviors
1. **Stderr capture**: Store last 100 lines of stderr for each failed gate
2. **Error classification**: Parse common failure patterns:
   - Compiler errors: extract file:line:col and error message
   - Test failures: extract test name and assertion message
   - Clippy warnings: extract lint name and suggestion
   - Format diff: extract files that need formatting
3. **Structured error field**: Add to GateResult:
   ```rust
   struct GateError {
       stderr_tail: String,       // last 100 lines
       error_count: Option<u32>,  // number of errors detected
       error_summary: Option<String>,  // one-line summary
       affected_files: Vec<String>,    // files mentioned in errors
   }
   ```
4. **Report integration**: Include GateError in the gates array of the JSON report

## Implementation notes
- Only capture stderr for failed gates (successful gates just store status+timing)
- The error classification is best-effort — don't try to parse all possible failure modes
- Regex patterns for Rust compiler errors: `^error\[E\d+\]: .*`
- Regex patterns for test failures: `^---- .* ----` or `thread '.*' panicked`
- Keep it simple: the goal is "actionable failure details", not a full error parser

## Location
crates/mcp-agent-mail-cli/src/ci.rs (GateError struct, enhance run_gate)
","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:28:13Z"},{"id":284,"issue_id":"br-1hei","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## SECOND-PASS NOTE: Self-contained tests for T1.5 and T1.6

These beads (T1.5 --parallel, T1.6 stderr capture) are intentionally NOT
in the T1.7 test dependency chain. This means they MUST include their own
unit tests within the implementation:

T1.5 should include tests for:
- Parallel execution produces same results as sequential
- NDJSON sidecar entries are still complete
- No interleaved output between gates

T1.6 should include tests for:
- Stderr is captured per-gate (not lost)
- stderr_output field appears in report JSON for failing gates
- Empty stderr for passing gates
- Large stderr is truncated if needed

These are ENHANCEMENT beads — the core test suite (T1.7) validates
the base functionality (T1.1-T1.4). The enhancement beads must validate
their own additions.
","created_at":"2026-02-12T02:06:51Z"}]}
 {"id":"br-1i11","title":"Epic: Deep Audit Hardening — defense-in-depth fixes from RubyPrairie code review","description":"## Background

RubyPrairie (claude-code/opus-4.6) performed a comprehensive 4-way parallel code audit of every crate in the workspace on 2026-02-08. The audit examined mcp-agent-mail-db (queries, schema, cache, pool), mcp-agent-mail-tools (messaging, identity, contacts, reservations, resources, search), mcp-agent-mail-server + mcp-agent-mail-storage, and mcp-agent-mail-core (config, metrics, backpressure, disk, identity).

Phase 1 (DONE, commit 3d096a2): Fixed the most critical findings — TTL overflow (saturating_mul across 8 sites), Config Debug redaction (http_bearer_token, http_jwt_secret), Negative TTL clamping (contacts, reservations), Unbounded search limit (clamped to 1..=1000), flush_sync timeout logging.

Phase 2 (THIS EPIC): Defense-in-depth hardening for all remaining findings. These were initially triaged as acceptable but each represents a real improvement opportunity that hardens the system against edge cases, aids debugging, and makes the codebase more robust for production use at scale.

## Overarching Goals

1. Deterministic behavior under concurrency — eliminate sources of non-determinism that make debugging harder (HashSet iteration, relaxed atomics without fences).
2. Defense in depth — validate inputs even when logically impossible to be invalid, because bugs in upstream code can propagate.
3. Observability — ensure silent failures are logged and metriced so operators can detect problems before users do.
4. Wire existing infrastructure — several caches and validation paths were built but never connected; wiring them completes the design intent.
5. Reduce blast radius of transient failures — DB errors during contact enforcement should not silently degrade security policy.","design":"Program decomposes into four orthogonal hardening tracks: (1) spill-path determinism, (2) contact-enforcement observability, (3) histogram snapshot consistency, (4) JWKS cache stampede protection. Each track must include implementation + unit/integration/e2e evidence + performance/safety analysis where relevant.","acceptance_criteria":"1) Every hardening track has granular implementation and validation subtasks. 2) No silent fail-open observability gaps remain uninstrumented. 3) Determinism/concurrency fixes are backed by repeatable stress tests. 4) E2E/log artifacts make production triage straightforward.","notes":"Scope boundary: this epic is orthogonal to dual-mode CLI/MCP planning (br-21gj*). Avoid cross-program coupling unless a concrete code dependency is discovered.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:35:42.384561250Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-08T22:39:45.973039809Z","closed_at":"2026-02-08T22:39:45.973019160Z","close_reason":"All 7 tracks closed (1-7): spill determinism, contact enforcement observability, histogram snapshot consistency, JWKS stampede protection, depth counter safety, thread_id defense-in-depth, inbox stats cache wiring. Full defense-in-depth audit complete.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["audit","defense-in-depth","hardening"],"comments":[{"id":36,"issue_id":"br-1i11","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Planning consolidation note (2026-02-08): reviewed all open hardening beads and merged duplicate subtasks into canonical workstreams. Closed duplicates: br-1i11.1.3, br-1i11.2.4, br-1i11.2.5, br-1i11.3.4, br-1i11.3.5, br-1i11.4.4, br-1i11.5.4, br-1i11.6.4, br-1i11.6.5, br-1i11.7.6, br-1i11.7.7, br-1i11.7.8. Added missing granular tasks for tracks 5/6/7 plus explicit dependency sequencing for deterministic execution.","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:44:19Z"},{"id":38,"issue_id":"br-1i11","author":"ubuntu","text":"Reopened: Reopened because child validation/e2e/benchmark beads remain open; keeping plan graph semantically consistent.","created_at":"2026-02-08T21:15:56Z"}]}
@@ -1036,7 +1036,7 @@
 {"id":"br-3nr5u","title":"Track B: Evidence Ledger Schema","description":"**Background**

The policy/threshold/adaptive domain has 2670 signal hits across the codebase, but all decision-making is currently ad-hoc: backpressure thresholds are hardcoded constants, TUI rendering strategies use magic numbers, cache TTLs are fixed. There is no structured record of why a particular decision was made, what the inputs were, or how the outcome compared to the prior.

**Graveyard reference:** Section 0.19 (Evidence Ledger). An evidence ledger is a structured, append-only log of decisions made under uncertainty. Each entry records: (1) the decision point identifier, (2) the state/evidence at decision time, (3) the action chosen, (4) the expected outcome, (5) the actual outcome (filled in later). This enables: Bayesian updating of priors, automated A/B testing, drift detection, and operator transparency.

**EV calculation:** (severity=4 * breadth=5 * feasibility=5) / (risk=2 * effort=2) = 25.0

This track is purely additive -- it does not change any existing behavior. It adds telemetry infrastructure that all subsequent tracks (D, G, H) depend on.","acceptance_criteria":"Acceptance criteria:
- EvidenceLedger struct implemented with JSONL emission and atomic append semantics
- At least 5 decision types wired into the ledger with typed payload schema and redaction policy
- Unit tests cover schema validation, serialization, rollover/rotation boundaries, and concurrent writes
- Integration tests verify record->outcome backfill and deterministic replay compatibility
- Galaxy-brain display widget reads live ledger entries and handles malformed/partial entries safely
- E2E scenario validates ledger output under load and confirms no behavior regressions when disabled
- tracing diagnostics include decision_id, chosen_action, confidence/posterior, and outcome correlation IDs","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-13T21:46:45.196698361Z","updated_at":"2026-02-14T00:41:23.194886866Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien","perf","tui"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-3nr5u","depends_on_id":"br-35pui","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T21:49:00.645975001Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-3nr5u","depends_on_id":"br-3hkkd","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T21:49:01.730455921Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-3nr5u","depends_on_id":"br-kqelk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T21:49:01.191311652Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-3o7hh","title":"R4.3: Implement am robot health — system diagnostics synthesis (probes, DB pool, disk, anomalies)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T02:17:19.140240211Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-12T05:21:46.268788357Z","closed_at":"2026-02-12T05:21:46.268764352Z","close_reason":"Implemented: robot health with 5 probes (db_connectivity, circuit_breakers, backpressure, integrity, disk), per-circuit status, alerts and actions","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-3o7hh","depends_on_id":"br-1nmyh","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T02:20:54.350093017Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":315,"issue_id":"br-3o7hh","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# R4.3: `am robot health`

## What
System health synthesis — equivalent to the SystemHealth TUI screen. Includes connection probes, diagnostics, and remediation hints.

## Data Collection Strategy
The CLI doesn't have a running server to probe. Instead:
1. **DB probe**: Open sync SQLite connection, measure latency
2. **Disk probe**: Check storage root exists, measure DB file size, archive size
3. **Process probe**: Check if mcp-agent-mail server is running on expected port
4. **Tool discovery**: Count tools available (from the tool registry in code)

## Implementation
```rust
fn robot_health(db_path: &str, storage_root: &str) -> HealthData {
    let probes = vec![];

    // DB probe
    let start = Instant::now();
    let db = open_db_sync(db_path);
    let db_latency = start.elapsed().as_millis();
    probes.push(HealthProbe { name: "database", status: "ok", latency_ms: db_latency, detail: None });

    // Disk probe
    let db_size = fs::metadata(db_path).map(|m| m.len()).unwrap_or(0);
    let archive_size = dir_size(storage_root);

    // TCP probe (check if server is running)
    let tcp_ok = TcpStream::connect_timeout(&addr, Duration::from_secs(1)).is_ok();

    // HTTP probe (if TCP ok, try unauthenticated request)
    if tcp_ok {
        // curl equivalent: GET /mcp/ → expect 401
    }
}
```

## Output Format
```
status: ok
health_level: healthy

probes:
  database: ok (3ms)
  disk: ok (db=12.4MB, archive=45.2MB)
  tcp: ok (port 8765 reachable)
  http: ok (401 Unauthorized — auth working)
  tool_count: 34

database:
  path: /home/user/.mcp_agent_mail/agent_mail.db
  size_mb: 12.4
  wal_size_mb: 0.1

disk:
  storage_root: /home/user/.mcp_agent_mail
  archive_size_mb: 45.2
  free_space_gb: 120.5

anomalies[0]:

_actions[0]:
```

## Health Level Classification
- **healthy**: All probes ok
- **degraded**: Some probes failed but DB accessible
- **error**: DB unreachable or critical failure

## Acceptance Criteria
- All probe types implemented
- Latency measured for DB probe
- Disk sizes reported in human-readable format
- Server running/not-running both handled gracefully
- Health level correctly derived from probe results
","created_at":"2026-02-12T02:28:25Z"},{"id":340,"issue_id":"br-3o7hh","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# R4.3: `am robot health`

## What
System health synthesis — equivalent to the SystemHealth TUI screen. Includes connection probes, diagnostics, and remediation hints.

## Data Collection Strategy
The CLI doesn't have a running server to probe. Instead:
1. **DB probe**: Open sync SQLite connection, measure latency
2. **Disk probe**: Check storage root exists, measure DB file size, archive size
3. **Process probe**: Check if mcp-agent-mail server is running on expected port
4. **Tool discovery**: Count tools available (from the tool registry in code)

## Implementation
```rust
fn robot_health(db_path: &str, storage_root: &str) -> HealthData {
    let probes = vec![];

    // DB probe
    let start = Instant::now();
    let db = open_db_sync(db_path);
    let db_latency = start.elapsed().as_millis();
    probes.push(HealthProbe { name: "database", status: "ok", latency_ms: db_latency, detail: None });

    // Disk probe
    let db_size = fs::metadata(db_path).map(|m| m.len()).unwrap_or(0);
    let archive_size = dir_size(storage_root);

    // TCP probe (check if server is running)
    let tcp_ok = TcpStream::connect_timeout(&addr, Duration::from_secs(1)).is_ok();

    // HTTP probe (if TCP ok, try unauthenticated request)
    if tcp_ok {
        // curl equivalent: GET /mcp/ → expect 401
    }
}
```

## Output Format
```
status: ok
health_level: healthy

probes:
  database: ok (3ms)
  disk: ok (db=12.4MB, archive=45.2MB)
  tcp: ok (port 8765 reachable)
  http: ok (401 Unauthorized — auth working)
  tool_count: 34

database:
  path: /home/user/.mcp_agent_mail/agent_mail.db
  size_mb: 12.4
  wal_size_mb: 0.1

disk:
  storage_root: /home/user/.mcp_agent_mail
  archive_size_mb: 45.2
  free_space_gb: 120.5

anomalies[0]:

_actions[0]:
```

## Health Level Classification
- **healthy**: All probes ok
- **degraded**: Some probes failed but DB accessible
- **error**: DB unreachable or critical failure

## Acceptance Criteria
- All probe types implemented
- Latency measured for DB probe
- Disk sizes reported in human-readable format
- Server running/not-running both handled gracefully
- Health level correctly derived from probe results

 {"id":"br-3oavg","title":"T9.3: Implement Reservation Create form","description":"Create a form for creating file reservations directly from the TUI.

FORM FIELDS:

- Custom TTL: TextInput (shown when Custom selected, format: Nh or Nm)
- Reason: TextInput (optional, e.g., "br-123")

KEYBINDING: 'n' (new) on Reservations screen opens form.

-{"id":"br-3ocb0","title":"I.2: SQL template caching with OnceLock","description":"**Background**

In `crates/mcp-agent-mail-db/src/queries.rs`, several hot-path functions build SQL strings using `format!()` on every call (e.g., line 3840-3843, 3897-3903, 3912-3917). The SQL template structure is stable -- only the number of placeholders varies. For fixed-arity queries, the template can be computed once and reused.

The string cloning issue in `run_like_fallback` (line 2197-2199) is also addressed here:
```rust
let escaped = format!("%{}%", like_escape(term));
params.push(Value::Text(escaped.clone()));  // unnecessary clone
params.push(Value::Text(escaped));
```

**Scope / Adoption wedge**

1. **OnceLock templates for fixed-arity queries**: For queries where the number of placeholders is known at compile time (e.g., `list_approved_contact_agent_ids` with a fixed IN clause), cache the SQL string in a `static` `OnceLock<String>`. Example:
   ```rust
   static APPROVED_CONTACTS_SQL: OnceLock<String> = OnceLock::new();
   fn approved_contacts_sql() -> &'static str {
       APPROVED_CONTACTS_SQL.get_or_init(|| {
           "SELECT b_agent_id FROM agent_links \
            WHERE a_project_id = ? AND a_agent_id = ? AND b_project_id = ? \
              AND status = 'approved' AND b_agent_id IN (?)"
           .to_string()
       })
   }
   ```
   Note: For variable-arity IN clauses, cache templates per arity (up to MAX_IN_CLAUSE_ITEMS).

2. **Eliminate string clone in LIKE fallback**: In `run_like_fallback` (line 2197-2199), the `escaped` string is cloned for the second parameter push. Instead, push the same format twice:
   ```rust
   let escaped = format!("%{}%", like_escape(term));
   params.push(Value::Text(escaped.clone()));
   params.push(Value::Text(escaped));
   ```
   This is already the pattern but can be optimized by using `Arc<str>` or by computing the two params separately. The simplest fix: keep the clone but wrap in `Value::Text` which likely already clones internally. Actually, looking at the code again: the `escaped.clone()` followed by `Value::Text(escaped)` is correct Rust -- the clone creates the first copy, then the original moves into the second. The "unnecessary clone" is actually necessary because two `Value::Text` instances are needed. The real optimization is to avoid the `format!` entirely for repeated terms.

   Better approach: For the WHERE clause building in LIKE fallback, pre-format the like pattern once and reference it:
   ```rust
   let patterns: Vec<String> = terms.iter().map(|t| format!("%{}%", like_escape(t))).collect();
   for pattern in &patterns {
       params.push(Value::Text(pattern.clone()));
       params.push(Value::Text(pattern.clone()));
   }
   ```
   This is the same number of clones but clearer intent.

3. **Template caching for variable-arity IN clauses**: Create a `PlaceholderCache` that stores precomputed placeholder strings for common arities:
   ```rust
   static PLACEHOLDER_CACHE: OnceLock<[String; 64]> = OnceLock::new();
   fn cached_placeholders(n: usize) -> &'static str {
       let cache = PLACEHOLDER_CACHE.get_or_init(|| {
           std::array::from_fn(|i| placeholders(i + 1))
       });
       if n > 0 && n <= 64 { &cache[n - 1] } else { /* fall back to dynamic */ }
   }
   ```

**Risks / Safe Mode**

- Risk: OnceLock initialization race (benign -- OnceLock handles this).
- Risk: Placeholder cache for arities > 64 falls back to dynamic allocation. This is the same as current behavior.
- Fallback trigger: None (pure optimization, no behavior change).

**Validation / Isomorphism proof**

- For every cached SQL template, `cached_template == format!(...)` for the same inputs. Verify by comparing in a test.
- For every cached placeholder string, `cached_placeholders(n) == placeholders(n)`. Verify by property test.

**Tests (5 required)**

1. `placeholder_cache_matches_dynamic` -- for n in 1..64, verify cached == dynamic
2. `sql_template_cache_consistent` -- verify OnceLock template matches format! output
3. `like_fallback_no_extra_clone` -- verify LIKE patterns are computed once per term
4. `template_cache_thread_safe` -- 10 threads access cache concurrently, no panic
5. `placeholder_cache_overflow_fallback` -- n=100 falls back to dynamic correctly","acceptance_criteria":"Acceptance criteria:
- OnceLock SQL templates and placeholder cache are implemented for fixed-arity hot paths
- Unit tests validate template generation, placeholder correctness, and LIKE pattern edge cases
- Integration tests verify query outputs and ordering remain unchanged after caching
- E2E regression scenario confirms search/contact flows behave identically with optimization enabled
- Performance benchmarks show lower allocation/formatting overhead on hot query paths
- Diagnostics include cache hit/miss for templates and fallback generation counts","notes":"## Plan-Space Review Findings (2026-02-13)

**CONCERN: Sprawling scope — 3 things in 1 bead.** Items (1) OnceLock templates and (3) PlaceholderCache are related but (2) LIKE optimization is separate. The LIKE section is confused — it starts by calling the clone "unnecessary", then walks it back ("actually necessary"), then proposes a pre-format approach with "the same number of clones." Cut the LIKE section or rewrite it clearly.

**CONCERN: LOW user benefit.** The `format!()` cost for SQL templates is negligible compared to actual SQL execution time (100x-1000x slower). The `PlaceholderCache` is a micro-optimization. OnceLock for static queries is fine but measurable impact is near zero. Consider deprioritizing to P3.

**FIX: LIKE clone IS necessary.** Lines 2197-2199: `let escaped = format!(...)` then `params.push(Value::Text(escaped.clone()))` then `params.push(Value::Text(escaped))`. Two `Value::Text` need two owned Strings. The clone is necessary. Remove the "unnecessary clone" framing.

**FIX: Add a benchmark.** This is a perf bead with no performance measurement. Benchmark `placeholders()` and `format!()` costs to validate the optimization is worth the complexity.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"TanHare","created_at":"2026-02-13T21:46:45.196698361Z","updated_at":"2026-02-14T08:27:54.836630040Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien","perf"]}

 {"id":"br-3ok6s","title":"T5.1: Implement TreeItem adapter for thread messages","description":"Create a TreeItem implementation that wraps thread message data for the frankentui Tree widget.

TREE ITEM STRUCTURE:
```rust
pub struct ThreadTreeItem {
    pub message_id: i64,
    pub sender: String,
    pub subject_snippet: String,
    pub relative_time: String,
    pub is_unread: bool,
    pub is_ack_required: bool,
    pub children: Vec<ThreadTreeItem>,
}

impl TreeItem for ThreadTreeItem {
    fn render(&self, area: Rect, buf: &mut Buffer, is_selected: bool, is_expanded: bool) {
        // Render: "▶ GoldHawk: Start implementation [2m ago]"
        // With unread = bold, ack_required = badge, selected = highlight
    }
    fn children(&self) -> &[Self] { &self.children }
}
```

Build the tree from flat message list:
- Messages with `reply_to_id` become children of the referenced message
- Messages without `reply_to_id` are root nodes
- Sort children by timestamp ascending

FILES: tui_widgets.rs, tui_screens/threads.rs","acceptance_criteria":"Acceptance criteria:
- [ ] TreeItem renders sender, snippet, time, badges
- [ ] Unread messages render bold
- [ ] Ack-required messages show badge
- [ ] Tree builds correctly from flat message list via reply_to_id
- [ ] Root messages sort chronologically
- [ ] Unit tests with nested reply chains

Plan-space hardening additions:
- [ ] Integration/E2E (or PTY/stress where applicable) scripts validate end-user flows, degraded-mode behavior, and recovery paths for this bead
- [ ] Detailed diagnostics artifacts are produced (scenario IDs, timing breakdowns, failure reason codes, artifact paths, and repro commands)","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:07:31.109806210Z","updated_at":"2026-02-14T04:36:51.570126949Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["adapter","tree-widget","tui"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-3ok6s","depends_on_id":"br-241rg","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T20:00:08.019186208Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-3ok6s","depends_on_id":"br-gtdw2","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:08:11.077756785Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-3op","title":"Share: bundle attachments (inline/detach/external/missing)","description":"## Objective
Implement share pipeline Step 8: attachment bundling and classification.

## Scope
- Thresholds: inline <= 64KiB, detach >= 25MiB (defaults, override via CLI).
- Classify attachments into inline (embed), detached (file), external (URL), missing.
- Generate attachment manifest entries with hashes and metadata.
- Honor `convert_images` and attachment embed policy.

## Tests
- Unit tests for threshold logic and classification.
- Integration tests with sample attachments (inline, large, external, missing).

## Logging/Artifacts
- Store attachment manifests and hashes under `tests/artifacts/share/attachments/<timestamp>/`.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Attachment classification matches legacy thresholds and rules.
2. Manifests include correct hashes and metadata.
3. Missing/external attachments are handled gracefully with clear markers.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-05T16:15:56.123691848Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-05T18:00:56.435100382Z","closed_at":"2026-02-05T18:00:56.435066689Z","close_reason":"Attachment bundling/classification already implemented in bundle.rs; bundle_* tests pass","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-3op","depends_on_id":"br-1uf","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-05T16:16:02.590128847Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-3p7th","title":"R4.2: Implement am robot metrics — tool performance summary (calls, errors, error%, latency percentiles)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T02:17:18.912768416Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-12T05:16:40.375602663Z","closed_at":"2026-02-12T05:16:40.375582285Z","close_reason":"Implemented: R4.2 robot metrics with per-tool calls/errors/latency, alert generation for high error rates and slow tools","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-3p7th","depends_on_id":"br-1nmyh","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T02:20:54.114144327Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":314,"issue_id":"br-3p7th","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# R4.2: `am robot metrics`

## What
Tool performance summary from the query tracking system. Shows agents which tools are slow, erroring, or heavily used. Uses the existing `tool_metrics_snapshot_full()` function that powers the TUI Tool Metrics screen.

## Data Source
Call `tool_metrics_snapshot_full()` which returns per-tool metrics:
- call_count, error_count, avg_latency_ms, p95_ms, p99_ms
- These are computed from the global QueryTracker singleton

## Output Format
```
summary:
  total_calls: 1234
  total_errors: 12
  error_rate: 0.97%
  avg_latency_ms: 45

tools[10]{name,calls,errors,error_pct,avg_ms,p95_ms,p99_ms}:
  fetch_inbox,450,0,0.0,12,25,45
  send_message,200,3,1.5,89,150,200
  search_messages,180,1,0.6,120,250,400
  file_reservation_paths,150,0,0.0,8,15,20
  acknowledge_message,100,0,0.0,5,10,15
  register_agent,50,2,4.0,30,50,80
  health_check,40,0,0.0,3,5,8
  summarize_thread,30,5,16.7,2500,4000,5000
  macro_start_session,20,1,5.0,150,300,500
  whois,14,0,0.0,6,10,15

_alerts[1]{severity,summary}:
  warn,summarize_thread has 16.7% error rate (5/30 calls)
```

## Alert Generation
- Tool with error_pct > 10% → warn alert
- Tool with avg_ms > 2000 → warn alert (slow tool)
- Tool with error_pct > 50% → error alert
- Overall error_rate > 5% → error alert

## Sorting
Default: sort by call count descending (most-used first).
Can be changed to sort by error rate or latency via --sort flag (future enhancement).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Summary section aggregates totals correctly
- Per-tool metrics match actual tracker data
- Alert thresholds trigger correctly
- Percentile calculation correct (matches existing p95/p99 logic)
- Works when metrics are empty (no tools called yet)
","created_at":"2026-02-12T02:28:24Z"},{"id":339,"issue_id":"br-3p7th","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# R4.2: `am robot metrics`
